author: @rjsongwriter
text: Would love to see further experiments with IR reflective paint, panels, or whatever. Since silicone is IR transparent and would allow IR in the atmospheric window range to be transmitted to space,, it seems the perfect binder for a calcium carbonate flexible tile, or shingle, made by diluting silicone caulk with naptha or spirits, adding the calcium carbonate, and pouring it into a mold.

author: @OllesMancave
text: TiO2 is normally to expensive to put in copy paper, even if some paper grades have it. Most copy papers have calcium carbonate only.

replies.author: @rjsongwriter

author: @RupertBruce
text: What's the argument for the insulation? My first instinct would be to spray the paper with water to get evaporative cooling. Put a black piece of paper above a white piece of paper and spray them both with water then put on an open-top-and-bottom chamber with a perspex lid which is pointed at the sun. Black is heated most, forcing the direction of air flow upwards. Extract the coolness of the white paper with a fan. Since the black paper is adding heat, it should be above you! Add a channel below the white-black edge to divert some cool air and magnify the pull of the black piece of paper, maybe...?

author: @hakkisuperheld
text: The flaw in your and his experiments is the use of an IR camera due to unknown but potentially different emission rates of the materials in the 7 to 14 micrometer range. You must use temperature measuring by contact probes to confirm a quantitative difference.

replies.author: @RupertBruce
replies.text: From a global perspective, the NighthawkInLight assertion was that emitting energy (presumably from any source...) into space through this 'hole' in the frequency spectrum would be a cooling effect as the energy is being 'dumped to ground' so measuring at that frequency measures the energy being ejected. To your point, the feeling of how hot it is to a human is measured with a wet and dry thermometer

replies.author: @hakkisuperheld
replies.text: @RupertBruce thanks for the answer. But nevertheless, in "that frequency" there are different emission rates for different substances. You can be easily tricked into taking your measurements serious wirh an IR camera. I do IR analysis for a living  and can assure that there is more to measurement than taking a microbolometer in your hands and read some values. Nontheless appreciate your work and your dedication.

author: @johnholmes8416
text:
When gas is compressed, it heats up rapidly. When compressed gas is released, it cools rapidly. 
Unless this statement is incorrect, it is fundamental to how a heat pump works. I am also trying to figure out how to stay cool in summer without the electrical grid. I need help from people smarter than myself. 
Please respond

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: the simple answers are often the best mate

replies.author: @johnholmes8416
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering compressing gas and releasing it is pretty simple I think.

replies.author: @johnholmes8416
replies.text: It's not just how a heat pump works, but refrigeration too, depending on how the gas is released. This technique has the ability to affect large temperature difference. That's why it's the industry standard. There must be a dozen ways of compressing gas without electricity.

Great little tip there Rob. I did try aluminium foil on the windows today (we had similar temperatures to you), and it worked well. Problem is the foil itself got rather hot. Next time, I'll have to try it on the outside of the windows!

This video, and my foil experimentation got me wondering though. I wonder if there'd be a way to come up with some kind of temperature reactive vinyl for windows (goes lighter with higher temperatures, and darker with lower ones). Had a good Google around, but can't spot anything useful.

Might be something to look into if that chemist brain of yours can think of a solution!

It will work better. Because if the foil gets hot and is inside the house, it will also heat the house.

author: @ryanlebeck259
text: There are apps you can get on your phone that shift the camera to the IR spectrum, and aside from that just use it as the cool side for a peltier unit and see if it will generate based on the temperature difference

replies.author: @technicalfool
replies.text: Those weird phone apps are entirely fake. However, you can get phones with thermal imaging sensors, and USB dongles with thermal imagers. They're usually based on one of the two Lepton sensors that FLIR do, and aren't all that pricey, but they're not casually-cheap either.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it's not just the app mate - which is basically a colour rendering app - for near infra red you need a camera click on addition, in addition most camera lenses these days have IR filters on the sensor - you might have guessed - I did look into this

replies.author: @technicalfool
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering Just as an aside, the phone I have is a 2018 edition Cat S61. It has the higher-resolution Lepton FLIR sensor in addition to a VOC sensor, humidity sensor, thermometer, and a laser-and-camera driven distance+area measurement app that's not exactly micron-accurate but good enough to be useful. Built like a tank as well. If you can pick one up new or second-hand at a decent price, you wouldn't even need a SIM in it to have something potentially useful.

Unfortunately the update doesn't have half the sensors in, which is a bit of a shame. That 2018 version really is a gadgetphone.

author: @winstonsmith478
text: He's using a thermal camera which indicates actual temperature.  You're using an IR thermometer where the emissivity of what you're measuring is an important factor.  See "Emissivity and Its Effect on Infrared Thermometer Readings."

replies.author: @technicalfool
replies.text: Thermal cameras are also affected by the same things that affect IR thermometers. When I point my own little FLIR-enabled phone at a window, the results look very strange because a lot of what the sensor sees is IR reflected from my side of the glass, with only some passing through from the other side, plus whatever IR signature the warm (or cold) glass itself has. This is why when you want to measure, for example, a metal container, you might want to stick some tape to the container so you get the temperature of the container rather than IR light reflecting off it.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate but FLIR is also effected by that

author: @OverlandOne
text: I would think the plastic cover sheet, which is not 100% transparent, might be blocking some of the reflectivity of the white paper possibly?  I saw Nighthawkinlight's video but I am still not clear on the need for the cover sheet.  It would be interesting to compare the Delta T at the same time using a cover sheet on one paper, and none on the other.

replies.author: @geodeaholicm4889
replies.text: the thin cover sheet of polypropilene is used to shield the i-r reflecting/emitting surface from convection & conductive heating from the moving air currents present nearby.

replies.author: @OverlandOne
replies.text: @geodeaholicm4889 Thank you.  Yes, I heard what Nighthawkinlight said it was for, I am just not sure it is worth the trade-off of losing the total available reflectivity of the paper.  This is why I think a side-by side comparison in the same sunlight at the same time would be interesting.  Maybe the plastic makes a huge difference?  I don't know.  I suppose I could run a few tests over here and find out.

replies.author: @geodeaholicm4889
replies.text:
@OverlandOne i would suspect the plastic film would primarily be useful during the heat of the day & in direct sunlight.
  i would think running multiple tests of multiple configurations would be useful.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: the best way to know that is to test it mate - give it a go and let us know the results

author: @kingofnothing2260
text: Black 3.0 on the hot side and super ambient temp reflecting white on the cool side of a sterling engine on a generator. replies.author: @gcewing
replies.text: If you paint a heatsink white it won't radiate very well. A good absorber of light is also a good radiator, and a poor absorber is aso a poor radiator. You would be better off painting the radiator black and shading it from the sun if necessary.

replies.author: @kingofnothing2260
replies.text: @gcewing perfect. Exactly what I meant. Ty
